title: Data Representation
summary: 9618 AS Computer Science — binary, denary, hex, two's complement, BCD, and ASCII/Unicode representation.
learningObjectives: Convert between binary, denary, and hexadecimal.
Represent signed integers using two's complement.
Explain BCD and when it is used.
Describe character encoding with ASCII and Unicode.

simpleExplanation.title: Numbers and text in bits
simpleExplanation.summary: Computers store everything as binary digits. Different formats encode integers, characters, and real numbers for processing and storage.
steps: Binary ↔ denary: place values 128, 64, 32… or repeated division for denary → binary.
Hex groups 4 bits — compact notation for memory dumps.
Two's complement: MSB = sign; negate by invert bits + 1.
ASCII 7-bit (128 chars); Unicode supports global scripts (UTF-8 variable length).

flashcards.back: Single addition circuit handles signed arithmetic.

sections.content: **Data representation** is the foundation of 9618 Paper 1. Cambridge sets **multi-step conversions**, **two's complement overflow**, and **floating-point** (A Level) — master 8-bit and 16-bit first.
Show **working** for conversions — partial marks are common. Watch **overflow** when adding two large positive two's complement numbers.

steps.caption: Denary ↔ binary using place value (powers of 2).
Hexadecimal: one digit = 4 bits — compact notation.
steps.embedHint: FF₁₆ = 255₁₀ — link to colour codes and memory dumps.

steps.caption: Characters stored as numeric codes (ASCII / Unicode).
Two's complement represents signed integers.
steps.embedHint: MSB = sign; invert bits + 1 for negative.
